question_en: How does getRandomBytesAsync(32) source entropy on a cheap Android device? If that device's hardware RNG is weak or poorly seeded, a documented issue on some budget chipsets, does your app check entropy quality before using that randomness to generate a private key that will custody real money?
answer_en: We use Expo's Crypto.getRandomBytesAsync, which delegates to the OS's own CSPRNG rather than anything we wrote ourselves, so entropy quality is exactly as good as the underlying Android or iOS implementation. We did not add a custom entropy-quality check on top; on a hackathon timeline we trusted the platform layer rather than re-auditing it ourselves, and that's a fair limitation to name.
question_ne: getRandomBytesAsync(32) le cheap Android device ma kasari entropy source garcha? Hardware RNG weak vaye, kunai entropy-quality check cha private key generate garna agadi?
answer_ne: Hami Expo ko Crypto.getRandomBytesAsync use garchau, jo OS ko aafno CSPRNG lai delegate garcha, hamile aafai banaeka hoina, so entropy quality underlying Android ya iOS implementation jasto nai huncha. Hamile separate entropy-quality check thapeka chainau, hackathon timeline ma platform layer lai trust garyaunau, aaphai audit garna sakaunau, ani yo fair limitation ho vannu parcha.

question_en: Is your bond ID sequential, or a UUID? If predictable, what stops an attacker from enumerating bond IDs and probing the sync endpoint to learn who owns what, without ever breaking a signature?
answer_en: Our bond IDs are generated as UUIDs, prefixed BOND, not sequential integers, specifically to avoid easy enumeration. That part we deliberately designed against this exact attack from the start.
question_ne: Tapaiko bond ID sequential ho ki UUID? Predictable vaye, attacker le bond ID enumerate garera sync endpoint probe garna sakcha ho, signature na todera pani?
answer_ne: Hamro bond ID UUID ho (BOND-uuid prefix sanga), sequential integer hoina, exactly yo enumeration attack avoid garna deliberately design gareko ho suruvat dekhi nai.

question_en: Ed25519 is not believed to be post-quantum secure. Given you're pitching this as critical financial infrastructure for a region that may not get app store updates reliably, what's your migration story the day curve-based signatures get formally deprecated?
answer_en: We don't have a post-quantum migration plan, and we'll say that plainly. Ed25519 was chosen for today's constraints, QR payload size and mobile CPU budgets, not as a twenty-year cryptographic bet. If this becomes a real product, a future client and server key-format versioning scheme would need to be designed before that becomes urgent; that's not something a hackathon team should pretend to have already solved.
question_ne: Ed25519 post-quantum secure mandainan. Curve-based signature formally deprecate vaye, tapaiko migration plan ke ho?
answer_ne: Hamisanga post-quantum migration plan chaina, yo clearly vanchau. Ed25519 ahile ko constraint (QR payload size, mobile CPU budget) ko lagi rojeko ho, 20-year cryptographic bet ko form ma hoina. Real product banaune vaye, key-format versioning design garnu parne huncha pachi, hackathon team le solve gareko vanya jhutho hunecha.

question_en: Your server private key signs every bond in existence. If it leaks through a misconfigured CI pipeline or a careless commit, every bond ever issued becomes forgeable. What's your actual incident response, and how fast can you physically push a new key to offline phones with no internet?
answer_en: Today, our incident response is manual and honestly immature for production, rotate the key, push an app update, and accept that already-offline users stay vulnerable until they update, which circles back to the rotation problem we admitted earlier. We don't have an automated kill-switch or a staged rollout system built yet, and we'd flag that as a priority before this leaves prototype stage.
question_ne: Server private key leak vaye, misconfigured CI ya committed .env bata, sabai bond forgeable hunecha. Incident response plan ke cha?
answer_ne: Ahile hamro incident response manual ho ani honestly production ko lagi immature, key rotate garne, app update push garne, ani offline vaisakeka users update samma vulnerable rahane vanya accept garne, jo pahile vaneko key-rotation problem sanga wapas jodincha. Automated kill-switch ya staged rollout system banaeka chainau, ani yo prototype stage bata agadi badhnu agadi priority ho vanya hamile maanchau.

question_en: The server's public key ships hardcoded inside a decompilable APK. Does anything else sensitive, API endpoints, test credentials, a Supabase service key, currently live in that same client bundle?
answer_en: We can open the repo and show you, the only credential embedded client-side is the public key, which is meant to be public by design. We did not embed Supabase service keys or test credentials in the mobile bundle; every privileged call requires a JWT issued server-side after authentication.
question_ne: Server ko public key client bundle ma hardcoded cha jo decompile garna milcha. Aru sensitive kura, API endpoint, test credential, Supabase service key, same bundle ma cha ki chaina?
answer_ne: Repo kholera dekhaunla, client side ma sirf public key matra cha, jo public huna nai design gareko ho. Supabase service key ya test credential mobile bundle ma embed gareka chainau; sabai privileged call ma authentication pachi server le issue gareko JWT chahincha.

question_en: Pooling user funds in escrow and earning float yield from partner banks is functionally a deposit-taking or e-money-issuing activity. Have you reviewed Nepal Rastra Bank's Payment Service Provider or Payment System Operator licensing framework, and do you believe a hackathon project can legally invest other people's money without that license?
answer_en: We have not done formal regulatory research with Nepal Rastra Bank, and we won't pretend we have, that's accurate scope for a hackathon prototype, not a launched fintech product. We do recognize that custodying pooled float and earning yield on it is exactly the kind of activity that would trigger PSP or PSO licensing, and any real path to market for BondPay almost certainly runs through partnering with an already-licensed bank or payment provider first, rather than us trying to become one ourselves.
question_ne: Pooled fund ma float yield earn garne kura deposit-taking ya e-money issuance jasto activity ho. Nepal Rastra Bank ko PSP/PSO licensing requirement research garnu vayo? Hackathon project le legally arko ko paisa invest garna milcha vanya believe garnu huncha?
answer_ne: Hamile formal regulatory research NRB sanga garaunau, yo honestly bhanchau, hackathon prototype ko lagi yo accurate scope ho, launched fintech product ko lagi hoina. Pooled float ma yield earn garne kura PSP/PSO licensing trigger garne activity ho vanya hami recognize garchau, ani real market path chai aafai PSP banne vanda existing licensed bank ya PSP sanga partner garera jaunu parcha vanya soch chau.

question_en: Foreign tourists are central to your pitch, but NRB foreign exchange regulations heavily restrict non-residents holding NPR-denominated digital wallet balances. How does a tourist legally get money into their BondPay online balance in the first place, before they ever reach the offline part of your demo?
answer_en: This is a genuine gap, we built the offline-spending half of the problem, not the cross-border onboarding half. In practice, our first real users would more likely be Nepali residents and small merchants topping up through existing licensed rails like a bank or an existing e-wallet, with the tourist use case needing a separate, compliant on-ramp we haven't designed yet.
question_ne: Tourist tapaiko pitch ko center ho, tara NRB ko foreign exchange rule le non-resident lai NPR digital wallet balance hold garna restrict garcha. Tourist le BondPay balance ma paisa kasari legally halne, offline part samma pugnu agadi?
answer_ne: Yo real gap ho hamro current design ma, hamile offline-spending half banayau, cross-border onboarding half hoina. Practically, hamro first real users probably Nepali resident ra small merchant hunecha jo existing licensed rail (bank ya e-wallet) bata top-up garchan, tourist use case ko lagi separate compliant on-ramp chahincha jo hamile design garyaunau abhi.

question_en: Your business model includes selling anonymized aggregate spending data to tourism boards. What is your actual consent mechanism, and have you considered that anonymized location-tied spending data from a small rural population is often trivially re-identifiable?
answer_en: That data-monetization idea is a forward-looking business-model sketch, not something we've built consent flows or anonymization pipelines for. You're right that small-population location data is a real re-identification risk, and we'd need a proper privacy review, explicit opt-in consent, and likely outside expertise on data protection before that line of business could responsibly exist.
question_ne: Tourism board ra logistics company lai anonymized data bechne plan cha. Consent mechanism ke ho, ani small population ko location-tied spending data re-identifiable hunsakcha vanya socha?
answer_ne: Yo data-monetization idea forward-looking business-model sketch ho, hamile consent flow ya anonymization pipeline banaeka chainau yo ko lagi. Tapai sahi hunuhuncha, small-population location data real re-identification risk ho, ani responsible huna proper privacy review, explicit opt-in consent, ani probably data protection expert chahincha yo line of business shuru garna agadi.

question_en: You say you'll use verified KYC data to pursue legal action against double-spenders. Where in your current signup flow do you actually collect a verifiable national ID or passport? Today you only ask for a phone number or email, that is not KYC, that is an account.
answer_en: You're correct, today's signup is phone or email only, that is account creation, not KYC. Any mention of using verified KYC data for legal enforcement was getting ahead of what we actually built. A real launch would need an actual identity verification step before that kind of claim is true.
question_ne: Double-spender against verified KYC data le legal action liney vanya cha. Tapaiko signup flow ma kaha real verifiable ID collect garnu huncha? Ahile ta phone ya email matra ho.
answer_ne: Sahi kura, ahile ko signup phone ya email matra ho, yo account creation ho, KYC hoina. KYC data use garera legal enforcement garne kura hamile banayeko vanda agadi ko kura ho. Real launch ko lagi actual identity verification step chahincha tyo claim sahi hune agadi.

question_en: AML and CFT frameworks generally require transaction monitoring close to real time. Your entire pitch is delaying settlement visibility by design, sometimes for days. Have you modeled how a regulator views a payment system whose core feature is reduced transaction-monitoring latency?
answer_en: We haven't modeled this against a regulator's lens, and that's a fair gap to flag. What we can say is that every transaction is still fully traceable once it syncs, nothing is anonymous, everything carries signed identities, nonces, and timestamps. The delay is in when monitoring happens, not whether it ever happens, but we agree that distinction would need to satisfy an actual regulator, not just us.
question_ne: AML framework le near-real-time transaction monitoring expect garcha. Tapaiko entire pitch settlement visibility delay garne ho, kei din ko lagi. Regulator ko lens bata yo model garnu vayo?
answer_ne: Hamile yo regulator ko lens bata model garyaunau, yo fair gap ho. Hami chai vannu chau ki sync vaisake pachi sabai transaction fully traceable cha, kei pani anonymous chaina, sabai ma signed identity ra timestamp cha, delay chai monitoring kaba huncha tyo ma ho, monitoring hudaina vanya kura hoina, tara yo distinction real regulator lai satisfy garna actual review chahincha, hami matra le vanya hudaina.

question_en: Walk me through the actual numbers: average offline bond load amount, average days outstanding before sync, and the resulting float. Multiply that by even an optimistic interest rate and tell me how many active users you'd need before that yield covers a single Supabase instance, let alone an engineering team.
answer_en: We haven't run those numbers with real assumptions, and presenting a figure we made up would be worse than admitting that. What we can say honestly is that float yield is one candidate revenue stream among several, and it stays unproven until we have real pilot data, average load size, average days outstanding, to build a financial model anyone should actually trust.
question_ne: Real number deu: average bond load amount, average days outstanding before sync, resulting float, realistic interest rate multiply garda kati active users chahincha ek Supabase instance cover garna?
answer_ne: Hamile real assumptions sanga yo number calculate garaunau, ani banaeko number dine vanda nabaneko vanya ramro ho. Honestly vanaau vane, float-yield idea hamro candidate revenue stream ho jasmadhye ek, real pilot data, average load size, average days outstanding, aaisakepachi matra financial model banauna milcha trust garna milne.

question_en: If you don't charge merchants or consumers a transaction fee, and float yield is your only declared revenue line, what happens to your runway in the realistic scenario where most users never let their balance sit idle for more than a day or two, because they loaded it specifically to spend it on a trek?
answer_en: That's a fair challenge to the whole revenue idea, and it's exactly why we'd treat float yield as unproven rather than a load-bearing assumption in a pitch. If real usage shows short hold times, the honest answer is the business model needs a different primary revenue line, possibly a small merchant-side fee, which we'd rather discover from real data than assume away today.
question_ne: Most users le balance lai short time matra rakhne vaye, float-yield revenue ko runway ke huncha?
answer_ne: Yo fair challenge ho, ani yehi reason ho hami float yield lai unproven treat garchau, pitch ko load-bearing assumption banaunu sahi hoina. Usage pattern short hold time dekhauncha vane, honest answer ho business model ko lagi different primary revenue line chahincha, sambhavatah small merchant-side fee, jo hami real data bata pata lagaunu chahanchau, assume garna vanda.

question_en: You're proposing to reimburse defrauded merchants from a platform reserve fund. How is that reserve fund capitalized before you have any revenue, and have you sized expected fraud losses against it, or is that number currently zero?
answer_en: As covered earlier, the reserve fund and reimbursement concept isn't implemented and we shouldn't have implied it was a current feature. Sizing it would require real fraud-rate data from an actual pilot before it's anything more than a hypothesis on a slide.
question_ne: Reserve fund kasari capitalize huncha revenue aaune agadi, fraud-rate size garnu vayo?
answer_ne: Pahile vaneko jasto, reserve fund ra reimbursement concept implemented chaina, ani hamile tyo current feature ho vanya imply garnu hudainan thiyo. Tyo size garna real pilot bata fraud-rate data chahincha, hypothesis vanda dherai hunu agadi.

question_en: Why would a tea house owner who already accepts Fonepay-interoperable QR codes from eSewa, Khalti, and half a dozen banks install a seventh, completely separate wallet app that only works if the customer in front of them happens to have installed the exact same app?
answer_en: That's a genuinely strong point, and we don't have a fully satisfying answer yet. The honest case for a standalone app at this stage is that it let us prototype the offline cryptographic core fast, without negotiating integration access to an existing interoperable rail. The long-term, more realistic path to actual adoption is probably licensing this as an offline-mode feature into an existing player like Fonepay, eSewa, or Khalti, rather than trying to win merchant installs from zero ourselves.
question_ne: Fonepay-interoperable QR already accept garne tea house le kina arko separate wallet install garla, jo exact same app vayeko customer sanga matra chalcha?
answer_ne: Yo genuinely strong point ho, hamisanga fully satisfying answer chaina abhi samma. Honest case standalone app ko lagi chai hamile offline cryptographic core fast prototype garna sakyau negotiate garna napari, tara tapai sahi hunuhuncha, long-term realistic path probably existing player (Fonepay, eSewa, Khalti) ma offline-mode feature ko form ma license garna ho, zero bata merchant install jitne vanda.

question_en: What is your actual customer acquisition plan for the sender side of the network? A tourist typically decides spontaneously where to spend money; expecting them to have pre-loaded a niche app's offline balance before reaching a specific tea house assumes a level of foresight your own example doesn't really support.
answer_en: We don't have a tested acquisition funnel, that's a real open question, not a solved one. Our best honest framing is that the natural entry point is probably trekking agencies or guesthouses pre-loading tourists' wallets as part of a package, rather than expecting spontaneous individual adoption, but we haven't validated that with real tourists.
question_ne: Sender side ko lagi acquisition plan ke ho? Tourist spontaneously kaha kharcha garne decide garcha, niche app ko offline balance pahile load garera aaune vanya assume garda foresight chai overestimate hoina ho?
answer_ne: Hamisanga tested acquisition funnel chaina, yo real open question ho, solved hoina. Honest framing chai trekking agency ya guesthouse le tourist ko wallet package ko part ko form ma pre-load garne natural entry point hunsakcha, individual spontaneous adoption expect garna vanda, tara yo hamile real tourist sanga validate garaunau.

category: Real-World Adoption and UX Reality Check
question_en: Your target merchant, a remote tea house owner, is exactly the demographic least likely to own a smartphone capable of reliably running a camera-based Expo app. Your own roadmap admits an SMS fallback for feature phones is still ninety days out. So functionally, who can use BondPay's offline mode today?
answer_en: Correct, today's offline mode only works on a smartphone capable of running the Expo app and camera, and our SMS fallback is genuinely just a roadmap idea, not built. Our realistic first users are smartphone-owning merchants near trekking routes, which does exist, but it's a smaller slice of the rural population than our pitch sometimes implies, and we should be upfront about that.
question_ne: Target merchant smartphone reliably chalauna sakne demographic hoina, ani SMS-fallback abhi 90 din roadmap matra ho. So ahile offline mode kasle actually use garna sakcha?
answer_ne: Sahi kura, ahile offline mode smartphone bata Expo app ra camera chalauna saknu parcha, SMS fallback genuinely roadmap idea matra ho, banaeka chainau. Hackathon ko lagi realistic first user trekking route najik smartphone-owning merchant ho, jo exist garcha, tara hamro pitch le kabhikahi imply garne rural population ko bhanda sano slice ho, ani hamile yo upfront vannu parcha.

category: Real-World Adoption and UX Reality Check
question_en: Your dual-QR handshake requires the merchant to actively operate a camera-scanning app mid-transaction. In bright outdoor daylight at altitude, common on trekking routes, phone screens are notoriously hard to read and touchscreens harder to use with gloves. Did you test this anywhere outside a conference room?
answer_en: We haven't field-tested this outside a demo setting, and that's an honest limitation, not something we can claim is solved. QR scanning libraries generally handle a fair amount of glare and motion blur, but gloved touchscreen interaction and altitude glare are exactly the kind of real-world conditions a pilot would need to validate before we'd trust this in production.
question_ne: Demo room bahira altitude ma bright daylight ra gloves sanga test garnu vayo?
answer_ne: Demo setting bahira field-test garaunau, yo honest limitation ho, solved vanya claim garna sakaunau. QR scanning library le glare ra motion blur jhainsamma handle garcha, tara gloved touchscreen interaction ra altitude glare jasta real-world condition pilot mai test garna parcha production trust garna agadi.

category: Real-World Adoption and UX Reality Check
question_en: What happens when the receiver has no confidence because their own screen shows a generic camera view with no progress indicator for several seconds while signature math runs, in a culture where digital payment trust is already fragile? Have you tested how long a real merchant is willing to stand there before assuming it failed?
answer_en: We haven't measured real merchant patience thresholds; what we can say is the actual measured signature verification time on a mid-range device is the honest number we have, and we're glad to demonstrate it live right now rather than quote an unverified figure.
question_ne: Merchant kati second samma wait garcha verify hune bela, before assume garcha fail vayo ani cash maagcha?
answer_ne: Hamile real merchant patience threshold measure garaunau; hami vanna sakne kura chai actual measured signature verification time, live run gareko, honest number ho, ani ahile nai demonstrate garna khusi chau, unverified figure quote garna vanda.

category: Real-World Adoption and UX Reality Check
question_en: Your hybrid split settlement answer for making change relies on the merchant happening to have the right denominations of bonds on hand. On a slow day with few transactions, what actually happens when neither party has exact change and the merchant has no fallback but physical cash, doesn't that just collapse back into the same cash-dependency you claim to be solving?
answer_en: You're right that our digital-change mechanism only works if the merchant happens to be holding the right denominations, and on a slow day it may genuinely fall back to handing over physical cash for the difference. We'd frame that as graceful degradation rather than failure, the larger payment still settles cryptographically and is fully recorded, only the small change reverts to old-fashioned cash, which is strictly better than the whole transaction failing.
question_ne: Digital change ko mechanism merchant sanga thik denomination bha vaye matra chalcha. Slow day ma neither party sanga exact change vaye ke huncha?
answer_ne: Sahi kura, hamro digital-change mechanism merchant sanga right denomination cha vane matra chalcha, slow day ma genuinely physical cash dinu parna sakcha difference ko lagi. Hami yo lai graceful degradation vannu chau, failure vanda, main payment cryptographically settle huncha ani fully record huncha, sirf sano change purano cash ma jancha, jo whole transaction fail huna vanda strictly ramro ho.

category: Real-World Adoption and UX Reality Check
question_en: If a phone is dropped in a river mid-trek, not stolen, just lost, and it held 2,000 NPR in unsynced offline bonds, is that money gone forever the moment the Secure Enclave key on that phone becomes unrecoverable?
answer_en: Yes, if the private key in that phone's secure storage is gone and the bonds were never synced, that value is genuinely unrecoverable today. That's a real, serious limitation of any token-based offline-bearer-asset design, ours included, and our honest mitigation is keeping the offline-bond cap deliberately low, our 3,000 NPR limit, so the maximum possible loss from a single lost device stays bounded and survivable, the same logic transit cards and prepaid limits use.
question_ne: Phone river ma fall vaye, unsynced bonds sanga, tyo paisa permanently gayab huncha?
answer_ne: Ho, private key secure storage bata gayab vaye ani bonds kabhi sync vayeka chainan vane, tyo value real ma unrecoverable ho ahile. Yo token-based offline-bearer-asset design ko serious real limitation ho, hamro pani, ani mitigation chai offline-bond cap (3,000 NPR) deliberately low rakhne ho, so ek device loss bata maximum possible loss bounded ani survivable rahos, airline ya transit card stored-value cap jasto logic.

question_en: Your redemptions table grows by one row for every single bond ever spent, forever, with no described archiving strategy. At national scale processing millions of small transactions a day, what's your plan before that table's index size starts degrading the very lock checks your double-spend defense depends on?
answer_en: We haven't built or tested any partitioning or archiving strategy, at hackathon scale our table has dozens of rows, not millions, so this genuinely hasn't been a problem we've had to solve yet, and we'd rather say so plainly than invent a scaling story we haven't built.
question_ne: redemptions table scale garda index size ko plan ke cha?
answer_ne: Hamile partitioning ya archiving strategy banaeka chainau ya test garaka chainau, hackathon scale ma hamro table ma dazens of rows matra cha, millions hoina, so yo real ma problem nai vayeko chaina abhi samma, ani scaling story banaaune vanda hami clearly vannu ramro maanchau.

question_en: Supabase's connection pooling has documented limits under high-concurrency row locking. If a popular merchant in a tourist hub is syncing dozens of transactions a minute during high season, have you load-tested your locking pattern under that contention, or is this purely theoretical?
answer_en: We have not load-tested under real concurrency, our locking pattern is correct in principle, but correct in principle and tested at scale are different claims, and we only have grounds to make the first one today.
question_ne: Real concurrency ma load-test garnu vayo?
answer_ne: Hamile real concurrency ma load-test garaunau, hamro locking pattern principle ma correct cha, tara principle ma correct ani scale ma tested alag claim ho, ahile hamisanga pahilo claim matra ko ground cha.

question_en: Your sync batch idempotency check queries a sync_batches table by batch ID on every single request. As that table grows into the millions of rows, what indexing strategy keeps that lookup fast, and have you benchmarked it at any scale beyond a hackathon demo?
answer_en: batch_id is the primary key on that table, so lookups are indexed by default in Postgres, which gives us a reasonable baseline. We haven't benchmarked it past hackathon volumes though, and we won't claim otherwise.
question_ne: sync_batches table millions row hune bela indexing strategy ke ho?
answer_ne: batch_id tyo table ko primary key ho, so Postgres ma default indexed nai cha, ramro baseline dincha, tara hackathon volume vanda dherai scale ma benchmark garaka chainau, alagai claim garaunau.

question_en: You're relying on a single centralized Node and Express instance for settlement. What's your actual disaster-recovery RPO and RTO if that instance or its underlying Postgres goes down mid-sync, not gracefully?
answer_en: We don't have a defined recovery objective, Supabase gives us managed backups out of the box, which is more than nothing, but we haven't designed or tested an actual disaster recovery runbook for this project yet.
question_ne: Server ya database mid-sync ma down vaye, recovery objective ke cha?
answer_ne: Hamisanga defined recovery objective chaina, Supabase le managed backup default dincha, jo kei nabhanda ramro ho, tara hamile actual disaster recovery runbook design ya test garaka chainau yo project ko lagi.

question_en: If an entire bus of trekkers reaches the first town with signal and all of their devices sync simultaneously against the same single cell tower, have you modeled the thundering-herd load that creates against your sync endpoint?
answer_en: We haven't modeled or load-tested this scenario. The exponential backoff in our sync service is a real, implemented pattern that does help smooth bursty retries, but we can't honestly claim it's been proven against a true thundering-herd event.
question_ne: Ek bus trekkers ek tower ma sync garda thundering-herd load model garnu vayo?
answer_ne: Hamile yo scenario model ya load-test garaka chainau. Hamro sync service ma exponential backoff real, implemented pattern ho jasle bursty retry smooth garna helps garcha, tara true thundering-herd event against proven cha vanya honestly claim garna sakaunau.

category: Demo Integrity and Proof of Work
question_en: Your developer logs screen showing color-coded crypto traces is built and rendered by your own team. What independent way can a skeptical judge verify those green checkmarks reflect real signature math and not a hardcoded UI state designed to look convincing on demo day?
answer_en: Fair challenge. The cleanest way to settle this is to let you watch the actual device console or network inspector live, not just our in-app log screen, while we run a real transaction in front of you right now. We're glad to do that.
question_ne: Log screen ko green checkmark real signature math ho ki hardcoded UI state, independently kasari verify garne?
answer_ne: Fair challenge, sabai vanda clean way chai actual device console ya network inspector live herna dine ho, sirf hamro in-app log screen hoina, real transaction run garda sangai. Ahile nai garna khusi chau.

category: Demo Integrity and Proof of Work
question_en: Can you deliberately tamper one byte of a bond's value field live, right now, in front of us, and show the rejection path firing, not just the success path you've rehearsed?
answer_en: Yes, let's do exactly that live, modify a bond value in the local database in front of you and show the receiver's verification fail in real time.
question_ne: Bond value ko byte live tamper garera rejection path dekhaunu hunchha?
answer_ne: Ho, garaau, local database ma bond value modify garera receiver ko verification real-time ma fail huni dekhaunchau.

category: Demo Integrity and Proof of Work
question_en: Open your actual GitHub repository right now, not a slide, and show us the literal file and line where crypto.sign() is called for bond issuance, so we can confirm this isn't a UI mockup wrapped around static fixture data.
answer_en: Happy to pull it up right now and walk through the exact line where crypto.sign() is called during bond issuance.
question_ne: Repo kholera signing function ko exact line dekhaunu hunchha?
answer_ne: Khusi le, ahile nai pull up garera crypto.sign() bond issuance ko bela kaha call huncha tyo line walk-through garaunchau.

category: Demo Integrity and Proof of Work
question_en: Put both devices in airplane mode, then physically force-kill one mid-handshake, right after the receiver scans but before they tap confirm. Show us, live, that no money was created or destroyed.
answer_en: We're glad to run that exact scenario live, kill the app right after the scan but before confirmation, and show the bond sitting in a pending state rather than vanishing or duplicating.
question_ne: Scan pachi tara confirm pahile app force-kill garera live test garnu hunchha?
answer_ne: Yo exact scenario live run garna khusi chau, scan pachi confirm agadi app kill garera bond pending state ma rahancha, gayab ya duplicate hudaina dekhaunchau.

category: Demo Integrity and Proof of Work
question_en: You claim signature verification completes in under 12 milliseconds via a native bridge. Run it ten times in front of us with a visible stopwatch and show us the actual numbers instead of the slide's number.
answer_en: We should retract the unverified twelve-millisecond figure since that assumed a native bridge we haven't built. What we'll do instead is time it live, right now, on the JavaScript implementation we actually have, and report whatever real number comes back.
question_ne: 12ms claim stopwatch sanga verify garnu hunchha?
answer_ne: 12ms ko unverified figure retract garnu parcha kinaki tyo native bridge assume gareko thiyo jo banaeka chainau. Live timing garaunchau actual JS implementation ma, jun number aaucha tyo honest number report garaunchau.

category: Edge Cases You Haven't Mentioned At All
question_en: What happens to a bond's value if a user changes their phone's region or locale settings to one with different decimal or currency formatting? Could a locale-driven parsing bug let a value of ten become misread as one thousand somewhere in your JavaScript layer?
answer_en: We haven't specifically tested locale-driven number parsing edge cases, that's a legitimate input-validation gap we haven't audited, and we'd flag it as a real to-do rather than claim it's handled.
question_ne: Phone ko locale different vaye decimal parsing bug hunsakcha ho?
answer_ne: Hamile locale-driven number parsing edge case specifically test garaunau, yo real input-validation gap ho jo audit garaka chainau, handled vanya claim garna vanda real to-do vanya flag garaunchau.

category: Edge Cases You Haven't Mentioned At All
question_en: What's the actual handling path if two different users, registering at nearly the same time, are issued colliding UUIDs by a misconfigured ID generator?
answer_en: We rely on UUID's astronomically low collision probability and a unique database constraint as the backstop, if a collision ever did occur, the insert would simply fail with a constraint violation rather than silently overwriting data, but we haven't written custom collision-recovery logic beyond that.
question_ne: Two users colliding UUID paye ke huncha?
answer_ne: Hami UUID ko astronomically low collision probability ani unique database constraint lai backstop ko form ma rely garchau, collision vaye, insert simply constraint violation sanga fail huncha, silently overwrite hudaina, tara custom collision-recovery logic banaeka chainau tesma badi.

category: Edge Cases You Haven't Mentioned At All
question_en: If a user's account is flagged and locked for fraud, what happens to bonds currently sitting, unspent, in other innocent people's wallets that originated from that now-frozen account? Are those bonds retroactively invalidated even though the people holding them did nothing wrong?
answer_en: Good catch, we haven't explicitly designed handling for this cascading scenario. Today, freezing an account stops that account from transacting further; it doesn't retroactively claw back bonds already legitimately passed on to other users, since those bonds were validly signed at the time they were issued. We think that's actually the more defensible default, but we haven't built explicit logic confirming either behavior.
question_ne: Frozen account bata already legitimately pass vayeka bonds, innocent users sanga vayeka, ke huncha?
answer_ne: Ramro point, yo specific cascading scenario ko lagi handling design garaunau. Ahile, account freeze garda tyo account le further transact garna roknu matra ho; already legitimately pass vayeka bonds claw-back hudainan, kinaki tyo bonds issue vaye ko bela validly signed thiye. Yo defensible default behavior ho vanya soch chau, tara explicit logic banaaunau either way.

category: Edge Cases You Haven't Mentioned At All
question_en: If a user reinstalls the app or buys a new phone, do they generate a brand-new keypair? Does the server allow a second public key to coexist for the same user ID, and if it does, doesn't that quietly break the one-owner-one-key assumption your entire ownership check is built on?
answer_en: This is a real unhandled case. Generating a new keypair on a new device and registering it would need explicit handling we haven't built, today our schema really only cleanly supports one active public key per user, and a second device would likely just overwrite that registration, which we recognize as a gap, not a feature.
question_ne: Naya phone vaye, naya keypair generate huncha? Server le second public key allow garcha ki?
answer_ne: Yo real unhandled case ho. Naya device ma naya keypair register garna explicit handling chahincha jo banaeka chainau, ahile schema ma ek user ko ek active public key matra cleanly support huncha, second device le tyo registration overwrite garna sakcha, jo hami gap vanya recognize garchau, feature hoina.

category: Edge Cases You Haven't Mentioned At All
question_en: Your design never addresses partial network connectivity, a connection too slow to complete an HTTPS handshake within a typical timeout, but technically online. Does your app correctly detect this degraded state, or does it falsely report online and then hang indefinitely trying to sync?
answer_en: expo-network reports radio-level connectivity, not actual reachability, so yes, a technically-connected-but-too-slow connection could report as online and then hang on a sync attempt. We haven't implemented a request-level timeout-and-fallback for that specific degraded state yet.
question_ne: 2G slow connection technically online dekhincha tara sync hang garcha ho?
answer_ne: expo-network le radio-level connectivity report garcha, actual reachability hoina, so technically-connected-tara-slow connection online dekhinsakcha ani sync attempt ma hang garna sakcha, hamile yo specific degraded state ko lagi request-level timeout-and-fallback implement garaunau.

question_en: You're asking rural Nepali merchants, many already wary of formal financial systems, to trust that an unspendable cryptographic promise is exactly as good as cash in hand until some future moment when a stranger's phone happens to find signal. Why should the burden of trusting new technology fall on the most financially vulnerable people in your entire user base?
answer_en: That's a fair moral challenge, and we don't think cryptography alone answers it, trust has to be earned through a real pilot with willing merchants who understand the tradeoff, backed by a real safety net like our velocity cap, not assumed just because the math works. We'd want any pilot to be opt-in, transparent about the bounded risk, and ideally backed by some small real guarantee before asking real merchants to rely on it for actual income.
question_ne: Vulnerable rural merchant lai naya technology trust garne burden kina dinu parne?
answer_ne: Fair moral challenge ho, ani hami sochne cryptography matra le yo answer dina sakdaina, trust real pilot bata earn garnu parcha, willing merchant haru sanga jo tradeoff bujhera ani velocity cap jasto real safety net sanga, math le work garcha vanya assume garera hoina. Hami chau ki kunai pilot opt-in, transparent bounded risk sanga, ani sambhavatah sano real guarantee sanga huna parcha real merchant lai income ko lagi rely garna vannu agadi.

question_en: Have you asked an actual rural tea house owner whether they're comfortable being the test case for that bounded fraud loss, or has that tradeoff only ever been validated by your own team?
answer_en: Not yet. Every tradeoff in this design has been validated by our own team's reasoning, not by an actual rural merchant's lived judgment, and that is exactly the next step before any real pilot, not something we can claim is already done.
question_ne: Yo tradeoff actual merchant le validate gareko cha ki?
answer_ne: Abhi samma chaina, yo design ko sabai tradeoff hamro aafnai team ko reasoning bata validate vayeko ho, real rural merchant ko lived judgment bata hoina, ani tyehi exactly next step ho real pilot agadi, gareko vanya claim garna sakine kura hoina.

question_en: If BondPay succeeds exactly as pitched, you're proposing that a private student project becomes the custodian of float for an entire underserved population's savings. What happens to that money, and to those users, the day your team graduates and moves on to other things?
answer_en: That's a legitimate concern with any student project that touches real money, and we don't have an answer beyond saying this prototype, as it stands, isn't ready to custody anyone's real savings. That would require a real legal entity, real licensing, and a real continuity plan, none of which exist yet at the hackathon stage.
question_ne: Team graduate vaye pachi yo paisa ko custody ke huncha?
answer_ne: Yo legitimate concern ho real paisa touch garne student project sanga, ani hamisanga answer chaina vannu vanda ki yo prototype, ahile ko form ma, real savings custody garna ready chaina, tyo lagi real legal entity, real licensing, ani real continuity plan chahincha, jo hackathon stage ma exist gardaina abhi.

question_en: You've built genuinely sophisticated cryptography to solve a problem that an already nationwide, already-trusted interoperable QR standard could arguably solve more simply by adding an offline cached-balance mode. Why does this need to be a brand-new wallet and not a feature pitch to an existing player?
answer_en: Honestly, the same point applies here as with the Fonepay question earlier, we don't have a fully satisfying answer. Building standalone let us move fast on the cryptographic core during a hackathon, but the more realistic long-term path is probably licensing this as a feature into an existing trusted rail rather than asking the market to adopt one more wallet from scratch.
question_ne: Kina existing Fonepay/eSewa/Khalti ma feature pitch garna vanda naya wallet banauna parne?
answer_ne: Sahi point, hamisanga fully satisfying answer chaina abhi. Standalone app banaune honest reason chai cryptographic core fast prototype garna ho, tara long-term realistic path probably existing player ma offline-mode feature license garera ho.

question_en: Strip away the cryptography for a second. At its core, isn't BondPay just IOUs between two phones that may or may not ever get honored by a server neither party controls in the moment? What, concretely, makes that meaningfully more trustworthy to an illiterate shopkeeper than simply trusting the tourist's word and settling up later?
answer_en: At the deepest level, yes, until settlement happens, it is two phones trusting math instead of trusting each other directly. What we'd argue is that's still meaningfully different from a verbal IOU, because the promise is cryptographically bound to a specific signed, server-issued token rather than just a person's word, so when settlement does happen, fraud is detectable and attributable in a way a handshake never is. We agree it does not eliminate the fundamental trust gap during the offline window itself, only make it provable after the fact.
question_ne: Cryptography hataidiyo vane, yo duita phone bich ko IOU matra ho jaslai server le honor garcha ki gardaina pakka chaina, ho ki haina?
answer_ne: Sabai vanda deep level ma, ho, settlement nahunda samma yo duita phone le ekarka lai trust garne vanda math lai trust garne kura ho. Hami vannu chau ki yo verbal IOU vanda meaningfully different ho, kinaki promise specific signed, server-issued token sanga cryptographically bound cha, vyakti ko word matra hoina, so settlement hune bela fraud detectable ani attributable huncha, jo handshake le kabhi garna sakdaina, tara offline window ko bela fundamental trust gap eliminate gardaina, settlement pachi provable matra banaucha, hami agree garchau.

question_en: What first gave you the idea for BondPay, was it a personal experience trekking or hearing about one?
answer_en: It came from noticing how often digital wallets fail exactly where they're needed most, places like trekking routes and even crowded parts of Kathmandu where signal drops without warning. It wasn't one dramatic story, it built up from several small frustrations each of us had separately noticed.
question_ne: BondPay ko idea kaha bata aayo, kunai personal trekking experience ho ki kunai sunya kura bata?
answer_ne: Yo idea digital wallet le exactly tyahi thau ma fail garne kura dekhera aayo jaha sabai vanda chahincha, jasto trekking route ra Kathmandu ko crowded area ma pani signal drop hune kura. Ek dramatic single story thiyena, hami sabai le aafai sano sano frustration bata build vayeko ho.

question_en: How long did your team actually spend building this prototype during the hackathon?
answer_en: We spent the better part of the hackathon window on this, working in parallel across the mobile app, the backend, and the crypto layer rather than building one piece at a time.
question_ne: Hackathon ma yo prototype banauna kati time laga'yo?
answer_ne: Hackathon ko dherai jaso time yehi project ma laga'yau, mobile app, backend, ani crypto layer parallel ma kaam garyau, ek ek garera hoina.

question_en: How did you split the work between the mobile app, the backend, and the cryptography?
answer_en: Roughly, one of us focused on the mobile client and the crypto signing flow, another owned the backend and the database, and the third worked on the security model and the business case, though all three of us crossed over wherever something needed extra hands.
question_ne: Mobile app, backend, ani cryptography ko kaam kasari baand'yeu?
answer_ne: Roughly, ek manche mobile client ra crypto signing ma, arko backend ani database ma, ani teshro security model ra business case ma focus garyo, tara jaha jaha extra help chahiyo tinai jana milera garyau.

question_en: What was your favorite part of building this for you personally?
answer_en: Probably watching the dual-QR handshake finally go green end-to-end for the first time, after a few rounds of failed signature mismatches that we had to debug one at a time.
question_ne: Yo project banaune bela tapaiko favorite part ke thiyo?
answer_ne: Probably dual-QR handshake first time end-to-end green hune dekhda, kei pahila signature mismatch sanga fail vairaheko thiyo, jo ek ek garera debug garyau.

question_en: What was the hardest bug you ran into while building the offline handshake?
answer_en: Getting the payload string used for the signature to be byte-for-byte identical between the frontend and backend was the toughest part, one extra space or a different field order completely breaks the signature, and that took us a while to track down.
question_ne: Offline handshake banaune bela sabai vanda hard bug ke thiyo?
answer_ne: Signature ko payload string frontend ani backend ma byte-for-byte same banauna sabai vanda hard thiyo, ek extra space ya different field order le signature totally todidincha, ani yo pata lagauna time laagyo.

question_en: Have you shown this to any actual shopkeepers or tourists yet, and what did they think?
answer_en: Not yet, beyond our own team and a few friends testing the demo. That's honestly the most important next step before we'd trust any of this further.
question_ne: Real shopkeeper ya tourist lai dekhaunu vayo ki, ke vannu vayo?
answer_ne: Hamro team ra kei friends bahek aru kahi dekhaeka chainau abhi samma. Tyo nai hamro next most important step ho yo lai aru trust garna agadi.

question_en: Why Ed25519 and React Native specifically, had any of you worked with these before?
answer_en: It came down partly to existing familiarity within the team and partly to research into what fits offline QR constraints best, Ed25519 keeps signatures small and React Native via Expo let us move fast across both Android and iOS in a short window.
question_ne: Ed25519 ra React Native kina specifically rojnu vayo?
answer_ne: Existing familiarity ani offline QR constraint ko research, duitai bata aayeko choice ho. Ed25519 le signature sano rakhcha ani React Native (Expo bata) le Android ra iOS duitai ma chito chito kaam garna diyo.

question_en: What's the very next feature you'd add if you kept building this after today?
answer_en: Probably the SMS fallback layer for feature phones, since that's the gap that excludes the most vulnerable users right now from the offline mode entirely.
question_ne: Aaja pachi continue garnu vaye next feature ke add garnu hunecha?
answer_ne: Probably SMS-fallback layer feature phone ko lagi, kinaki tyehi sabai vanda vulnerable users lai offline mode bata completely exclude garne gap ho ahile.

question_en: Could this same idea work for something other than payments, like loyalty points or tickets?
answer_en: Yes, the same signed-token idea could plausibly work for anything that's a bounded-value, transferable claim, loyalty points, transit or event tickets, even small inventory vouchers.
question_ne: Yo idea payments bahek loyalty point ya ticket jasto kura ko lagi pani chalna sakcha?
answer_ne: Ho, yehi signed-token idea loyalty point ya transit ticket jasto bounded-value, transferable claim ko lagi pani chalna sakcha.

question_en: How did you come up with the name BondPay?
answer_en: Bond as in a financial bond, a written promise of value that can be redeemed later, plus a nod to the two phones bonding directly with each other during the handshake.
question_ne: BondPay naam kasari aayo?
answer_ne: Bond financial bond jasto, lekhit promise of value jo pachi redeem garna milcha, plus duita phone bich handshake bata bond garne idea pani.
